games.explanation: The market is pricing Miami like the lesser team here, and the actual gap on the field runs the other direction. Pittsburgh is rolling out a young arm who can be lifted by left-handed bats, with a walk rate north of eleven percent against lefties, and Miami's lineup has the personnel to make him work. Alcantara isn't the version that won a Cy Young, but he's a competent floor against a Pirates order that hasn't scared anyone all season. Getting plus money on the better Elo team in a projected one-run game is the entire angle, and +122 is more than enough to cash the ticket.

Bryce Miller is being priced like an ace by a market that hasn't bothered to check whether he's actually getting outs, and the answer is mostly no. Lefties and righties alike are squaring him up at a clip that belongs to a back-end starter, which is bad news with James Wood waiting in the three-hole to do exactly what Miller's hard-hit numbers suggest he'll allow. Littell isn't a star but he pounds the zone, walks nobody, and gives Washington the kind of stable floor that turns a coin-flip game into a live dog. The sim has this within a tenth of a run, and the board is giving you +125 to take the side that profiles better at the plate. That's the whole pitch.

Joe Ryan's surface line looks like an ace until you notice every dangerous bat in the Cardinals lineup hits from the wrong side for him. Ryan is a different pitcher against lefties, and St. Louis happens to roll out Burleson and a few other left-handed problems behind Herrera, who has been demolishing pitching from both sides at a .371 xwOBA clip. Leahy isn't flashy, but a Busch night game with crosswinds is exactly the kind of environment where Buxton's barrels die on the warning track. Getting a plus price on the better team in a coin-flip game is the entire angle, and +119 is doing the heavy lifting.

Tampa Bay as a favorite makes sense until you remember they have to hand the ball to someone, and tonight that someone is throwing to a lineup built to punish exactly his problem. Sam Aldegheri walks the ballpark, but the Angels stacking Trout and Adell against a lefty who can't find the zone is the kind of mismatch the market keeps shrugging at. Aldegheri has issued walks to nearly one in five hitters he's faced, which is the sort of number that turns a 4-4 game into a 6-4 game in a hurry. The sim has this as a coin flip; the board is pricing it like a clear Rays edge, and +144 on the wrong side of that gap is the entire pitch.

Ryan is a different pitcher against lefties, and St. Louis happens to roll out Burleson and a few other left-handed problems behind Herrera, who has been demolishing pitching from both sides at a .371 xwOBA clip. Leahy isn't flashy, but a Busch night game with crosswinds is exactly the kind of environment where Buxton's barrels die on the warning track. Tampa Bay as a favorite makes sense until you remember they have to hand the ball to someone, and tonight that someone is throwing to a lineup built to punish exactly his problem. Sam Aldegheri walks the ballpark, but the Angels stacking Trout and Adell against a lefty who can't find the zone is the kind of mismatch the market keeps shrugging at. Aldegheri has issued walks to nearly one in five hitters he's faced, which is the sort of number that turns a 4-4 game into a 6-4 game in a hurry. The sim has this as a coin flip; the board is pricing it like a clear Rays edge, and +144 on the wrong side of that gap is the entire pitch.

The market is pricing this like Tampa is the clearly better club running into a soft Angels arm, and the first part is true but the second part is the trap. Aldegheri is going to walk the yard and probably give up a couple of loud ones, but the Rays don't actually need to score eight to win, and our sim has this landing inside a run. Neto, Trout and Adell are a tough top-three for any righty to navigate twice, and Trout in particular has been the better hitter against right-handed pitching this year by a comfortable margin. When the projected final is 4.3 to 4.2, you don't lay the runs, you take them at -114 and let the one-run math do the work.

The market is pricing this like the Athletics are supposed to walk a bad Rockies team off the field, but Oakland hasn't been that team in months and Colorado covers run lines for a living when they're getting a hook. The sim sees this finishing inside a run, with Goodman in particular giving the A's staff problems given his barrel rate climbs into the high teens against arms he should be seeing tonight. A projected 4.8 to 4.5 final isn't a game you lay chalk into, it's a game you take the cushion and the plus money. Bad teams cover spreads against mediocre ones, and -105 on a 59.6% number is the trade you make every time.
